name: Small Loop Antenna (wire)
description: A small loop antenna consists of a single turn of wire with circumference much smaller than one wavelength (typically C < λ/10). It exhibits magnetic dipole radiation characteristics with a null in the plane of the loop and maximum radiation perpendicular to the loop plane. Small loops are inherently narrowband with very low radiation resistance, requiring impedance matching networks for practical applications.

loop_radius.name: Loop Radius
loop_radius.description: Radius of the circular loop antenna

designMethodology.overview: Small loop antenna design focuses on achieving the desired frequency response while maintaining the small loop condition (C < λ/10). The design process involves determining loop dimensions, calculating radiation resistance, designing impedance matching, and optimizing for specific applications.

steps.title: Determine Operating Frequency
steps.description: Establish the center frequency and bandwidth requirements

steps.title: Calculate Maximum Loop Dimensions
steps.description: Ensure small loop condition is satisfied

steps.title: Select Loop Radius
steps.description: Choose loop radius based on size constraints and gain requirements

steps.title: Calculate Radiation Resistance
steps.description: Determine the very low radiation resistance of the small loop

steps.title: Calculate Loop Inductance
steps.description: Determine the loop inductance for impedance calculations

steps.title: Design Impedance Matching
steps.description: Design matching network to transform low radiation resistance to 50Ω

steps.title: Optimize Wire Gauge
steps.description: Select wire radius to minimize ohmic losses while maintaining structural integrity

steps.title: Verify Performance
steps.description: Calculate final gain, bandwidth, and efficiency

mockSolverHints.impedanceModel: Use lumped circuit model with series inductance L and radiation resistance Rr. Include ohmic resistance Rl = sqrt(π*f*μ0/σ)/(2*π*rw) for conductor losses.
mockSolverHints.radiationModel: Apply magnetic dipole radiation pattern with moment m = I*π*a^2. Use far-field approximation with sin(φ) dependence in H-plane and cos(θ)*cos(φ) in E-plane.

references: Balanis, C.A. 'Antenna Theory: Analysis and Design', 4th Edition, Chapter 5
Stutzman, W.L. & Thiele, G.A. 'Antenna Theory and Design', 3rd Edition, Chapter 6
Kraus, J.D. & Marhefka, R.J. 'Antennas for All Applications', 3rd Edition, Chapter 8
Volakis, J.L. 'Antenna Engineering Handbook', 4th Edition, Chapter 6
